senses.definition: a state characterized by great excitement or agitation

examples.x: The atmosphere in the stadium is reaching a fever pitch as the teams battle it out in the final minutes of the game.
The political rally is at a fever pitch as passionate supporters express their views with fervor.
The crowd's excitement reached a fever pitch when the home team scored the winning goal.
Emotions ran at a fever pitch during the intense debate as both sides passionately argued their points.
Anticipation is building, and the excitement is expected to reach a fever pitch at the highly anticipated music festival next week.
The campaign's momentum is building, and support is growing at a fever pitch.
The crowd was in a state of fever pitch, cheering and chanting throughout the entire concert.

word_list.pronunciation: ɡɛt ɔːɹ sˈɛt wˈʌnz pˈʌls ɔːɹ hˈɑːɹt ɹˈeɪsɪŋ

examples.x: The intense car race sets my pulse racing every time I watch it.
The thrilling roller coaster ride always gets my heart racing.
The suspenseful movie got my pulse racing as I anxiously waited to see what would happen next.
The upcoming concert is going to get my pulse racing with excitement.
The adventure trip next week will definitely set our pulses racing.

senses.definition: in a way one desires without being concerned about the future
to demonstrate signs of anxiousness, anger, or frustration in difficult situations

examples.x: I feel like pulling my hair out when my computer crashes right before an important presentation.
She often tears her hair out trying to manage her busy schedule and meet all her deadlines.
He pulled his hair out when he realized he had lost the keys to his car.
We were pulling our hair out during the hectic holiday shopping season.

senses.definition: in a very nervous or excited state because of something that is going to happen

examples.x: She is on pins and needles as she awaits the arrival of her job interview invitation.
He was on pins and needles before the final exam, fearing he hadn't studied enough.
We were on pins and needles while waiting for the decision on our loan application.
They will be on pins and needles until the winner of the competition is announced.

word_list.word: like a cat on a hot tin roof
word_list.pronunciation: lˈaɪk ɐ kˈæt ˌɑːn ɐ hˈɑːt tˈɪn ɹˈuːf

senses.definition: used to describe someone who is in a very agitated state of mind

examples.x: She's pacing back and forth, like a cat on a hot tin roof, unable to sit still before her important presentation.
He was like a cat on a hot tin roof when he was waiting for the exam results, unable to relax until he received them.
I'll be pacing around the room, like a cat on a hot tin roof, until I hear back about the job interview.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv ɡɛt bˈʌɾɚflˌaɪz ɪn wˈʌnz stˈʌmək

senses.definition: to be really excited or nervous about what is going to happen

examples.x: She has butterflies in her stomach before every performance, but it fuels her passion on stage.
They always have butterflies in their stomachs when speaking in public, but they manage to deliver confident presentations.
He had butterflies in his stomach throughout the entire competition, but his hard work paid off with a victory.
We had butterflies in our stomachs before the big exam, but we were relieved when it was finally over.

senses.definition: an intense feeling of suspense, fear, or nervousness

examples.x: She will have her heart in her mouth as she awaits the results of her medical test, hoping for good news.

senses.definition: a sense of anxiety or urgency, because there is a lot that one must do quickly

examples.x: Everyone is in panic stations as the fire alarm blares throughout the building.
They always go into panic stations when the deadline for their project approaches.
We went into panic stations when the storm hit and the power went out.
He was in panic stations as he realized he had misplaced his passport right before the trip.
She will be in panic stations trying to finish her presentation before the important meeting.

senses.definition: to have a hard time staying calm due to being extremely nervous or excited about something

examples.x: He has ants in his pants as he eagerly waits for his favorite band to take the stage.
They always have ants in their pants when it's time for the annual family vacation.
She had ants in her pants while waiting for the exam results, unable to sit still.
They will have ants in their pants as they anticipate opening their presents on Christmas morning.
I will have ants in my pants waiting for the job interview outcome.
The children are having ants in their pants, running around the playground with excitement.
He's been having ants in his pants all day, eagerly counting down the minutes until the party starts.

senses.definition: to have a tendency to become worried over something that has not yet happened

examples.x: Sarah tends to meet trouble halfway by constantly worrying about worst-case scenarios that may never happen.
Mark met trouble halfway last week when he panicked over a minor work issue that never materialized.
At this rate, Brad will continue meeting trouble halfway by imagining all sorts of problems before any arise.
Jane is meeting trouble halfway right now as she stresses over potential fallout from a situation that has yet to play out.
By the time the big presentation rolled around, Alan had already met trouble halfway so many times that he was exhausted from unnecessary worrying.
By next month, I predict Claire will have met trouble halfway so frequently that her anxiety is out of control.

senses.definition: having a strong and urgent desire to do something

examples.x: She's hot to trot and can't wait to start her new business venture.
They're hot to trot for tonight's concert and have been counting down the days.
He was hot to trot when he got the opportunity to present at the conference.
We were hot to trot for the competition, having trained tirelessly for months.
They will be hot to trot for the upcoming job interview, fully prepared and confident.

senses.definition: to behave in an angry, violent, or crazy way, particularly due to overwhelming stress

examples.x: With how stressed Bob has been at work lately, I wouldn't be surprised if he went postal and snapped at his coworkers.
Remember that time Jeff went postal and got fired for threatening his boss during a meeting?
At this rate, the long hours might cause Sarah to go postal and lose her job if she doesn't find a healthier way to deal with burnout.
Ever since he got passed over for the promotion, Mark has been acting very strangely and I worry he's going postal in the office.
By the time security arrived, the disturbed employee had already gone postal, barricading himself in his cubicle with a weapon.
I hope they implement more employee assistance programs to prevent anyone from having snapped so much they've gone postal by next quarter.

word_list.word: like a chicken with its head cut off
word_list.pronunciation: lˈaɪk ɐ tʃˈɪkɪn wɪð ɪts hˈɛd kˈʌt ˈɔf

senses.definition: used to describe someone who is extremely confused and excited

examples.x: She was so overwhelmed with work that she was darting from one task to another like a chicken with its head cut off, without any clear plan or organization.
As the deadline approached, the team was working frantically, each member moving like a chicken with its head cut off, trying to complete their assigned tasks.

word_list.pronunciation: ʃˈɪvɚɹ ɔːɹ tʃˈɪl dˌaʊn ɔːɹ ˌʌp wˈʌnz spˈaɪn

senses.definition: a sudden physical reaction to a strong emotional experience, such as fear, excitement, or awe, typically characterized by a cold or trembling sensation running down the back

examples.x: The eerie silence in the abandoned house sends a chill down my spine.
The chilling revelation sent a shiver down her spine, leaving her stunned.

word_list.pronunciation: kˈʌm fˈɔːl ɐpˈɑːɹt æt ðə sˈiːmz

senses.definition: to experience a breakdown or failure due to overwhelming stress or pressure

examples.x: The project is coming apart at the seams due to poor communication and constant changes.
She feels like her life is coming apart at the seams with the overwhelming demands of work and personal responsibilities.
The team's plans came apart at the seams when key members resigned unexpectedly.
After months of tension, their marriage fell apart at the seams, leading to a painful divorce.
If we don't address the underlying issues, this fragile alliance will come apart at the seams.
The old building is in such disrepair that it will eventually fall apart at the seams if left unattended.

senses.definition: in a very  troubled or nervous state
to be really stressed, angry, or upset about something that has happened or is going to happen

examples.x: She's going to have a cow when she finds out we accidentally broke her favorite vase.
Every time I mention spiders, my sister has a cow and starts screaming.
When I told him I lost his wallet, he had a cow and yelled at me for hours.
The boss had a cow when he saw the report full of errors.
If you don't finish your homework, your teacher is going to have a cow.
I can already imagine Dad having a cow when he sees the dent on his car.
